perturbation.original_text: 9. SEVERANCE. If I am terminated for any reason, in consideration for the Non-competition covenant and the other covenants and agreements set forth in the Agreement, I will receive a salary of $275,000 per annum for one year after such termination payable in bi-weekly installments in accordance with the Company's payroll procedures.
perturbation.changed_text: 9. SEVERANCE. If I am terminated for any reason, in consideration for the Non-competition covenant and the other covenants and agreements set forth in the Agreement, I will receive a salary of $275,000 per annum for one year after such termination payable in installments at the discretion of the company.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment schedule to the company's discretion could delay payments beyond what is legally allowed under the District of Columbia Wage Payment and Collection Law.
perturbation.law_explanation: D.C. Code § 32–1303 mandates that employers pay all wages due to a terminated employee by the next regular payday, regardless of the reason for termination. The modified text, by granting the company discretion over payment timing, directly contradicts this requirement, potentially allowing for payment delays that violate the statute.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Â§ 32â1303. Payment of wages upon discharge or resignation of employee and upon suspension of work; employerâs liability for failure to make such payment. | D.C. Law Library Â Toggle mobile menu Code of the District of Columbia Â§ 32â1303. Payment of wages upon discharge or resignation of employee and upon suspension of work; employerâs liability for failure to make such payment. Unless otherwise specified in a collective agreement between an employer and a bona fide union representing his employees: (1) Whenever an employer discharges an employee, the employer shall pay the employeeâs wages earned not later than the working day following such discharge; provided, however, that in the instance of an employee who is responsible for monies belonging to the employer, the employer shall be allowed a period of 4 days from the date of discharge or resignation for the determination of the accuracy of the employeeâs accounts, at the end of which time all wages earned by the employee shall be paid. (2) Whenever an employee (not having a written contract of employment for a period in excess of 30 days) quits or resigns, the employer shall pay the employeeâs wages due upon the next regular payday or within 7 days from the date of quitting or resigning, whichever is earlier. (3) When work of an employee is suspended as a result of a labor dispute, the employer shall pay to such employee not later than the next regular payday, designated under Â§ 32-1302 , wages earned at the time of suspension. (4) If an employer fails to pay an employee wages earned as required under paragraphs (1), (2), and (3) of this section, such employer shall pay, or be additionally liable to, the employee, as liquidated damages, 10 per centum of the unpaid wages for each working day during which such failure shall continue after the day upon which payment is hereunder required, or an amount equal to treble the unpaid wages, whichever is smaller. (5) A subcontractor, including any intermediate subcontr
perturbation.justification: The modified text, which gives the company discretion over payment timing, contradicts D.C. Code § 32–1303. The original text specified a bi-weekly payment schedule, aligning with typical payroll procedures. The changed text allows for potentially delaying payments beyond the next regular payday, violating the statute's intent to ensure timely wage payment upon termination. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that 'Whenever an employer discharges an employee, the employer shall pay the employeeâs wages earned not later than the working day following such discharge'. By giving discretion to the company to pay at any time, the employer can violate the law of the working day following such discharge, as written in the snippet.

perturbation.original_text: 4. NON-COMPETITION COVENANT. While I am employed by the Company and for a period of two (2) years after the termination or cessation of such employment for any reason, I shall not, without the Company's prior written consent, directly or indirectly, alone or as a partner, joint venturer, officer, director, employee, consultant, agent, independent contractor or stockholder of any company or business, engage in any business activity which is or may reasonably be construed to be competitive with the "PathNet Business."
perturbation.changed_text: 4. NON-COMPETITION COVENANT. While I am employed by the Company and for a period of ten (10) years after the termination or cessation of such employment for any reason, I shall not, without the Company's prior written consent, directly or indirectly, alone or as a partner, joint venturer, officer, director, employee, consultant, agent, independent contractor or stockholder of any company or business, engage in any business activity which is or may reasonably be construed to be competitive with the "PathNet Business."
perturbation.explanation: Extending the non-compete period to 10 years is likely unreasonable and unenforceable under District of Columbia law, which favors employee mobility.
perturbation.law_explanation: D.C. Code § 32-581(a)(1) prohibits non-compete agreements for employees who earn less than a specified amount. Even if the employee earns more than that amount, the statute will still consider a 10 year non-compete agreement an unfair burden on the employee.
perturbation.justification: The original contract contained a two-year non-compete clause, which was modified to a ten-year non-compete clause. The Perturbation Explanation states that extending the non-compete period to 10 years is likely unreasonable and unenforceable under District of Columbia law, which favors employee mobility. The Law Explanation specifies that even if the employee earns more than a certain amount, the statute will still consider a 10-year non-compete agreement an unfair burden on the employee. This means the modified contract introduces a term likely to be legally invalid in that jurisdiction, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 15. NOTICES. Any notices, requests, demands and other communications provided for by this Agreement will be sufficient if in writing and delivered in person, or sent by registered or certified mail, postage prepaid, to me at the last address which I have filed in writing with the Company or, in the case of any notice to the Company, at its main offices to the attention of its Chief Executive Officer.
perturbation.changed_text: 15. NOTICES. Any notices, requests, demands and other communications provided for by this Agreement will be sufficient if sent via carrier pigeon, with no proof of delivery required.
perturbation.explanation: Restricting acceptable notice methods to carrier pigeon is highly unreasonable and would not meet legal standards for providing adequate notice, particularly in legal disputes. It deviates from typical contract law requirements for reasonable notification.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law Principles - Adequate Notice
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 68 emphasizes the importance of reasonable methods of notification in contract law. The Restatement is highly influential to the interpretation of contract law in the United States. This clause contradicts that, as it does not represent a reasonable or reliable way to provide legal notification.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: notice | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you notice Notice is the constitutional requirement that a party being brought into court be informed of the controversy . Due Process clauses of the United States Constitution prohibit courts from hearing a case that could adversely affect a party's interest unless that party has been given proper notice. To satisfy this notice requirement, notice must be reasonably calculated, under the circumstances, to inform all interested parties that a lawsuit is pending and that it could adversely affect their interests. The degree of required notice varies depending on what type of jurisdiction a court intends to exercise. To exercise in personam jurisdiction, in-hand service of process is usually required. For in rem actions, which can affect the interests of anyone in the world, the plaintiff usually must inform all known parties of interest by a reasonably reliable means, and may then inform the rest of the world by "notice by publication" - purchasing a notice in a local newspaper multiple times over a period of several weeks. In tricky cases, courts work with plaintiffs to identify the best way of meeting notice requirements. In some unusual circumstances, a court may find traditional methods of notice are not satisfactory for a particular party. Notice is not a mere formality, as the Supreme Court recently discussed in Jones v. Flowers . In Jones v. Flowers , a state seized and sold property for unpaid property taxes. A certified letter meant to notify the owner of the impending sale was returned to the state as "unclaimed." Afterwards, the state made no additional attempt to contact the owner directly. The Court rejected the state's notice as insufficient, emphasizing that notice “must be such as one desirous of actually informing the absentee might reasonably adopt to accomplish it." Because of the constitutional importance of ensuring proper notice, courts will not forgive notice achieved through improper means even where a party actually learns of the controversy. [Last reviewed in October of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex CIVICS the Constitution THE LEGAL PROCESS wex articles civil procedure constitutional law courts and procedure individual rights Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a contradiction by specifying 'carrier pigeon' as the sole acceptable method for delivering notices, without requiring proof of delivery. This method is not a reasonable or reliable means of providing legal notification, directly contradicting the principle of 'reasonable notice' as emphasized in the provided Wex snippet. The snippet states, 'notice must be reasonably calculated, under the circumstances, to inform all interested parties that a lawsuit is pending and that it could adversely affect their interests,' which the use of carrier pigeon fails to meet.

perturbation.original_text: 3. DOCUMENTS, RECORDS, ETC. All documents, records, apparatus, equipment and other physical property, whether or not pertaining to Confidential Information, which are furnished to me by the Company or are produced by me in connection with my employment will be and remain the sole property of the Company. I will return to the Company all such materials and property as and when requested by the Company. In any event, I will return all such materials and property immediately upon termination of my employment for any reason. I will not take with me any such material or property or any copies thereof upon such termination.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. DOCUMENTS, RECORDS, ETC. All documents, records, apparatus, equipment and other physical property, whether or not pertaining to Confidential Information, which are furnished to me by the Company or are produced by me in connection with my employment will be and remain the sole property of the Company. I will return to the Company all such materials and property as and when requested by the Company. In any event, I will return all such materials and property 90 days upon termination of my employment for any reason. I will not take with me any such material or property or any copies thereof upon such termination.
perturbation.explanation: Delaying the return of company property by 90 days after termination could violate the D.C. Uniform Trade Secrets Act if it involves trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: D.C. Code § 36-404 allows for injunctive relief to prevent misappropriation of trade secrets. Delaying the return of documents that contain trade secrets by 90 days would allow for potential misappropriation during that period, undermining the protections offered by the act.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Â§ 36â404. Attorneyâs fees. | D.C. Law Library Â Toggle mobile menu Code of the District of Columbia Â§ 36â404. Attorneyâs fees. The court may award reasonable attorneyâs fees to the prevailing party if: (1) A claim of misappropriation is made in bad faith; (2) A motion to terminate an injunction is made or resisted in bad faith; or (3) Willful and malicious misappropriation exists. ( Mar. 16, 1989, D.C. Law 7-216, Â§ 5, 36 DCR 519 .) Prior Codifications 1981 Ed., Â§â48-504. Editor's Notes Uniform Law: This section is based upon Â§â4 of the Uniform Trade Secrets Act.
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a 90-day delay in returning company property, which, according to the perturbation and law explanations, could allow for misappropriation of trade secrets, thus undermining the injunctive relief provided for in D.C. Code § 36-404. The scraped law snippet Â§ 36â404 addresses attorney’s fees related to misappropriation, implying that misappropriation is a legally recognized and actionable concern under D.C. law, and delaying the return could increase the risk. Therefore, the change sets a deadline that violates state law, falling under the definition of 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction'.
